danger_signals.signal: Rising blocks approach top of playfield
danger_signals.visual_form: Tetrominoes stack visually higher in the play grid; screen fills from bottom upward
danger_signals.meaning: Imminent game over; player losing control of play space

danger_signals.visual_form: Pieces fall faster with each level increase; visual tempo increases
danger_signals.meaning: Game difficulty escalating; at level 29+ becomes 'kill screen' (unplayably fast)

danger_signals.visual_form: Pieces fall at maximum speed (1 grid cell per frame); level numbers display improperly past 29
danger_signals.meaning: Game approaching intentional crash point; designed endpoint where developers never expected play to continue

safety_signals.signal: Cleared lines and high score accumulation
safety_signals.visual_form: Score display in top-right corner increases; lines cleared counter increments
safety_signals.meaning: Player performing well; maintaining control and efficiency

safety_signals.visual_form: Preview window shows upcoming tetromino adjacent to playfield
safety_signals.meaning: Player has information advantage; can plan ahead safely

safety_signals.visual_form: Game pieces change color palette with each new level (levels 1-10 and beyond)
safety_signals.meaning: Safe progression; game advancement communicates through aesthetic shift

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Saint Basil's Cathedral appears prominently in title screen and ending sequences; alludes to Soviet origin of Tetris by Alexey Pajitnov
Nintendo characters perform in front of cathedral after high-level B-Type completion; institutional branding through character cameos

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Slower piece drop speed; playable speed with normal reflexes; piece colors cycle through palette changes
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player can react naturally; game reward structure emphasizes score accumulation efficiency

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Moderate speed increases at specific levels (13, 16, 19); playfield becomes increasingly pressured visually
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must develop technical skill; speed plateaus between increases allow mastery windows

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Pieces fall at maximum frame-locked speed; level display becomes corrupted/improper; visual chaos
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game becomes unplayable by design; modern players use novel speed techniques (rolling controller) to bypass intended endpoint; game crashes as designed endpoint

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Animated rocket launch in front of Saint Basil's Cathedral; rocket size scales with final score (bottle rocket → Buran spaceplane → UFO); cathedral lifts off at maximum score
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Aesthetic reward for score efficiency; visual spectacle communicates achievement magnitude

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Nintendo character performance in front of cathedral after clearing 25 lines
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Character cameo serves as institutional reward; celebratory visual closure

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Playfield visually fills to top; blocks can no longer fall
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game terminates; in competitive mode (Nintendo World Championships 1990) cannot restart

environmental_storytelling.example: Saint Basil's Cathedral as constant backdrop in title and endings explicitly signals Tetris' Soviet origin and cultural significance; transforms puzzle game into geopolitical narrative

Rocket launch animation grows from bottle rocket (small score) to Buran spaceplane (high score) to UFO with cathedral ascension (maximum score); score efficiency directly communicates progression narrative

environmental_storytelling.technique: Visual speed ramping as difficulty storytelling
environmental_storytelling.example: Piece drop speed acceleration tells implicit narrative of mounting pressure; level-specific color palette shifts mark temporal progression without explicit text
Nintendo character appearances in B-Type victories establish institutional presence and celebrate player achievement through character recognition
Tetromino counter panel tracks piece frequency (visual right panel showing appearance counts); next piece preview communicates game state information through UI placement

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Controlled descent; player can reliably rotate and position; visual drop speed allows reaction time
Faster descent with periodic speed jumps at level milestones; requires anticipatory rather than reactive play
Maximum speed (1 cell per frame); falls faster than human reflexes can process; effectively uncontrollable by traditional means
Level display becomes visually corrupted; game not designed to display levels past 29; signals intentional endpoint
Crashes due to inefficient multiplication operations; software behaves erratically; this crash is popularly considered 'beating the game'

notes: Tetris NES is a minimal-text game that communicates world-state entirely through visual and behavioral manifestation. The game's 'danger' is abstract (mathematical speed escalation) rather than literal threats. The Soviet/cultural framing through Saint Basil's Cathedral provides geopolitical subtext without explicit narrative. The 'kill screen' at level 29 represents intentional design closure—the developers never expected play beyond this point, making the game itself a bounded system. Modern competitive play has discovered methods to overcome this boundary, effectively rewriting the game's implicit ending. The ending animations scale with score, making the visual reward directly proportional to play efficiency. No factions compete; instead, institutional presence (Nintendo characters) is celebratory rather than adversarial. The game communicates all narrative and state information through pure visual and mechanical language.
